content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 1, 1991

Philip R. Recht
Manatt, Phelps & Phillips
11355 West Olympic Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90064

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-153

Dear Mr. Recht:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform
Act. I have not identified the person on whose behalf you are
seeking advice. Therefore, we are treating your letter as a
request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c) (copy enclosed).² On March 14, 1991, I telephoned you for
additional information concerning your letter of March 6, 1991.
You rephrased the question as follows:

QUESTION

Under the Political Reform Act, must an elected officeholder
establish a separate committee to raise unlimited funds for ballot
measure activities?

CONCLUSION

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a candidate or
officeholder who has established a committee to support his or her
own candidacy from raising funds into that account for ballot
measure activities. The fiscal year contribution limitations that

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Philip Recht
I-91-153
Page 2


were added to the Act by Proposition 73 have been invalidated.³
However, the Act's contribution limits with respect to special
elections and special runoff elections remain in effect.  (Section
8105.)  Furthermore, if the committee is controlled by a person
who holds office in a locality which has a local ordinance impos-
ing valid contribution limits, the officeholder would be under the
jurisdiction of the local ordinance.  The district attorney of
each county or the city attorney would interpret local ordinances.

     Expenditures from campaign funds must be reasonably related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose unless they
confer a substantial personal benefit, in which case such
expenditures must be directly related to a political, legislative,
or governmental purpose.  (Section 89512.)  An officeholder may
expend campaign funds from his or her election account to support
or oppose a ballot measure as long as such expenditures are
reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Sandra L. Taylor
                                             Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure




_________________________
³ Service Employees International Union v. Fair Political
Practices Commission, Case No. CIVS 89-0433 (LKK-JFM).  The deci-
sion in this case is being appealed.

sections.question: Under the Political Reform Act, must an elected officeholder establish a separate committee to raise unlimited funds for ballot measure activities?
sections.conclusion:
The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a candidate or officeholder who has established a committee to support his or her own candidacy from raising funds into that account for ballot measure activities. The fiscal year contribution limitations that

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

were added to the Act by Proposition 73 have been invalidated. However, the Act’s contribution limits with respect to special elections and special runoff elections remain in effect. (Section 85305.) Furthermore, if the committee is controlled by a person who holds office in a locality which has a local ordinance imposing valid contribution limits, the officeholder would be under the jurisdiction of the local ordinance. The district attorney of each county or the city attorney would interpret local ordinances.

Expenditures from campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose unless they confer a substantial personal benefit, in which case such expenditures must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) An officeholder may expend campaign funds from his or her election account to support or oppose a ballot measure as long as such expenditures are reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

(Section 89512.) An officeholder may expend campaign funds from his or her election account to support or oppose a ballot measure as long as such expenditures are reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
